explicit_text: I became involved with the Rainbow Coalition around 1984-1986, initially through my role as a Jesse Jackson delegate in Spokane County. The organization was deeply rooted in grassroots social movement politics, and in Washington State, it was the most organized branch in the country. I participated in protests, such as those at the South African consulate in Seattle, and later served on the steering committee of the Washington State Rainbow Coalition into the early 1990s. We organized statewide, with chapters from Vancouver to Bellingham and east of the Cascades, focusing on diverse communities and progressive causes.

explicit_text: I moved to Washington state in 1977 after growing up in Ohio. I attended graduate school at Washington State University from 1977 to 1984 and later lived in Seattle and Bellingham. Washington was the central place for my political activism, especially with the Rainbow Coalition, where we organized across the state from Vancouver to Whatcom County and even east of the Cascade Range. The state’s political landscape during the 1980s, including events like the 1988 caucuses and conventions in Olympia, shaped much of my involvement.

explicit_text: I grew up in Ohio and lived there until I was twenty-five years old before moving to Washington in 1977. My early life and formative years were spent there, but my political activism and graduate studies happened after I relocated. Ohio was the place I came from before engaging deeply in the Rainbow Coalition and electoral politics in Washington.

explicit_text: I attended graduate school at Washington State University from 1977 until 1984. During that time, I lived in Spokane County and became involved in local politics as a Jesse Jackson delegate in 1984. My experience at WSU and in Spokane helped launch my engagement with the Rainbow Coalition and the broader political movement in Washington State.

explicit_text: Jesse Jackson was the central figure around whom the Rainbow Coalition was built. I first became active as a Jackson delegate in 1984 during his presidential primary campaign and continued to support him in his 1988 run. Jackson's campaigns inspired grassroots organizing across Washington State, and I served on the advisory committee for his 1988 campaign in Washington. Even after he endorsed Michael Dukakis, Jackson kept the movement alive, and I remained involved in the Rainbow Coalition's activities.
implicit_text: The civil rights leader I supported was the central figure around whom the grassroots coalition was built. Even after he endorsed the Democratic nominee, he kept the movement alive, and I remained involved in the coalition's activities.

explicit_text: I lived in Spokane County before moving to Seattle and was active there as a Jesse Jackson delegate during the 1984 Democratic Convention. I was the sole Jackson delegate elected from my caucus and helped rally support to maximize delegate numbers at the county and state conventions. Spokane County was my initial base of political activism in Washington State before relocating.

Spokane County was my initial base of political activism in the state before relocating.

explicit_text: After moving from Spokane County, I settled in Seattle where I became involved with the Rainbow Coalition and met key activists like Larry Gossett and Robby Stern. Seattle was a hub for protests, including the regular demonstrations at the South African consulate in 1985-86, which I and my wife attended. The city was central to the Rainbow Coalition's organizing efforts and progressive activism during the 1980s.

explicit_text: In 1985-86, the Rainbow Coalition organized weekly protests at the South African consulate in Seattle, pushing for economic sanctions against apartheid South Africa. I and my wife began attending these Sunday afternoon demonstrations, where leaders like Larry Gossett were often arrested for trespassing. These protests were a significant part of the Rainbow Coalition's activism during that period in Seattle.

explicit_text: Larry Gossett was a key leader in the Washington State Rainbow Coalition and someone I met after moving to Seattle. He led protests at the South African consulate and was often arrested during those demonstrations. I invited Larry to speak at Western Washington University in 1987 and worked alongside him organizing for Jesse Jackson's 1988 campaign. Larry was later elected president of the Rainbow Coalition at its founding convention in Olympia in 1989.

Larry was later elected president of the coalition at its founding convention in Olympia in 1989.

explicit_text: Robby Stern was a labor organizer active in King County and Washington state politics during the mid-1980s. I met him through the Rainbow Coalition community in Seattle around 1985-86. Robby was part of the network of activists involved in progressive causes and the Jesse Jackson campaign that I engaged with during that time.

explicit_text: I taught political science at Western Washington University from the mid-1980s until 2021. While living in Bellingham, I invited Larry Gossett to speak to my class in 1987 and helped organize a dinner meeting to introduce colleagues to the Rainbow Coalition. The university served as a base for political education and organizing during Jesse Jackson’s 1988 presidential campaign in Washington State.

explicit_text: I lived in Bellingham while teaching at Western Washington University during the late 1980s and beyond. It was from there that I helped organize support for Jesse Jackson’s 1988 campaign and the Rainbow Coalition. Bellingham was part of the broader Whatcom County area where we had significant delegate support during the 1988 caucuses.

explicit_text: In 1988, Washington State held its caucuses on Super Tuesday in March, which was a critical moment for Jesse Jackson’s presidential campaign. We organized weekly meetings leading up to the caucuses to train and prepare Jackson supporters, growing from about 15-20 people to 30-40 attendees. The caucuses were a complex process, and our efforts helped secure half the delegates from Whatcom County for Jackson.

explicit_text: Whatcom County was a stronghold for Jesse Jackson supporters during the 1988 caucuses, where we managed to secure half the delegates despite inexperience with local political horse trading. My wife Rebecca Johnson and I were both Jackson delegates to the state convention representing Whatcom County. The county was home to one of the Rainbow Coalition’s local chapters that I helped found and organize.

explicit_text: Olympia was the site of the 1988 Washington State Democratic Convention where Jesse Jackson supporters, including myself and Rebecca Johnson, demonstrated to show our strength within the party. It was also where the Rainbow Coalition held its founding convention in the winter of 1989, during a snowy President’s Day weekend. Olympia served as the political center for major events and organizing milestones in the state.

explicit_text: Michael Dukakis was the Democratic nominee in 1988, and although Jesse Jackson did not win the nomination, he endorsed Dukakis. I was involved in the Rainbow Coalition advisory committee during the 1988 campaign, and we continued to support the movement even after Jackson backed Dukakis. Dukakis’s nomination marked the end of the primary campaign but not the end of our activism.

explicit_text: Larry Gossett, a former member of the Black Panther Party, was a key leader in the Washington State Rainbow Coalition. The Black Panther Party’s legacy of grassroots activism and social justice informed much of the coalition’s approach to organizing and political engagement. This connection helped root the Rainbow Coalition in a tradition of militant and community-based activism.
